objects.name: Recorded Future
objects.description: Recorded Future is the publisher of this threat intelligence report. STIXed by TI Mindmap HUB.

objects.name: TI Mindmap HUB
objects.description: TI Mindmap HUB is an AI-powered threat intelligence platform that generates STIX bundles from OSINT sources.

objects.name: Exploitation of React Server Components Deserialization Flaw (React2Shell)
objects.description: Threat actors globally exploited CVE-2025-55182, a deserialization vulnerability in Meta's React Server Components, enabling unauthenticated remote code execution (RCE) on servers running React and Next.js. China-nexus groups (Earth Lamia, Jackpot Panda, UNC clusters) and North Korea-linked actors leveraged public proof-of-concept code to rapidly compromise targets. Attackers used this flaw to deploy diverse payloads, including EtherRAT, PeerBlight, ransomware, and Cobalt Strike stagers, often pivoting to further internal systems. The exploitation was highly automated, with relay infrastructure (HiddenOrbit, GobRAT) facilitating command-and-control and lateral movement. This technique allowed initial access without authentication, making it a preferred entry vector in December 2025.

objects.name: Deployment of Diverse Malware via React2Shell
objects.description: Following successful exploitation of the React2Shell vulnerability, threat actors deployed a wide array of malware families, including EtherRAT, PeerBlight, CowTunnel, ZinFoq, Kaiji variants, Zndoor, RondoDox, MINOCAT, SNOWLIGHT, COMPOOD, HISONIC, ANGRYREBEL.LINUX, and Weaxor ransomware (using a Cobalt Strike stager). This campaign was notable for its use of multiple payloads, enabling remote access, data exfiltration, and ransomware operations. Attackers tailored payload selection to target environment characteristics, maximizing persistence and impact. The deployment was often automated and chained with relay infrastructure for stealthy post-exploitation activities.

objects.name: Use of Relay Infrastructure for Command and Control
objects.description: Threat actors established command and control channels using specialized relay infrastructure, notably HiddenOrbit and GobRAT components. After initial compromise, these relays facilitated encrypted communications between infected hosts and attacker-controlled servers, enabling remote management, lateral movement, and payload delivery. The infrastructure provided redundancy and evasion capabilities, complicating detection and remediation efforts. Relay usage was observed across multiple malware families deployed in the React2Shell campaign, supporting both espionage and financially motivated operations.

objects.name: Exploitation of Legacy Vulnerabilities for Initial Access
objects.description: Threat actors renewed exploitation of older vulnerabilities (2018-2022 era), as highlighted by CISA’s addition of these flaws to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog. Attackers targeted unpatched systems with weaknesses such as unrestricted file uploads (CWE-434), path traversal (CWE-22), and buffer overflows (CWE-120), gaining initial access to networks that failed to remediate legacy issues. This technique demonstrates persistent threat actor interest in historical flaws, leveraging public PoC code where available to automate exploitation and expand attack surface.

objects.name: Exploitation of Cryptographic Signature Verification Flaws
objects.description: Critical vulnerabilities in Fortinet products (CVE-2025-59718, CVE-2025-59719) allowed attackers to bypass authentication mechanisms due to improper verification of cryptographic signatures. Threat actors exploited these flaws to gain privileged access to affected devices, often without user interaction. Public proof-of-concept code accelerated exploitation, enabling rapid compromise of enterprise appliances and facilitating further lateral movement or deployment of malicious payloads.

objects.name: Malware Deployment via Gogs Path Traversal Vulnerability
objects.description: Unknown threat actors exploited CVE-2025-8110, a path traversal vulnerability in Gogs, to deploy Supershell malware. By leveraging public exploit code, attackers bypassed directory restrictions, writing malicious files to sensitive locations on the server. This enabled remote access and persistence, with Supershell providing attackers with control over compromised systems for further operations. The technique was used for targeted attacks against organizations running vulnerable Gogs instances.

objects.name: Exploitation of Cisco Secure Email Gateway for Shell Deployment
objects.description: The UAT-9686 threat actor exploited CVE-2025-20393 in Cisco Secure Email Gateway, leveraging improper input validation to deploy AquaShell, AquaPurge, and AquaTunnel malware. This allowed attackers to execute arbitrary commands and establish persistent backdoors within enterprise email infrastructure. The exploitation facilitated both espionage and data exfiltration operations, with malware providing remote access and tunneling capabilities.

objects.name: Abuse of Hard-coded Credentials in Gladinet CentreStack and Triofox
objects.description: Attackers exploited CVE-2025-14611, which involved the use of hard-coded credentials in Gladinet CentreStack and Triofox products. By leveraging publicly available exploit code, threat actors gained unauthorized access to sensitive systems, bypassing authentication controls. This technique enabled attackers to escalate privileges, deploy malware, and exfiltrate data from compromised environments.

objects.name: Automated Exploitation via Public Proof-of-Concept Code
objects.description: A key feature of December’s threat landscape was the widespread use of public proof-of-concept (PoC) exploit code. Eleven of the twenty-two actively exploited vulnerabilities had PoCs available, enabling threat actors to automate exploitation across diverse targets. This accelerated attack timelines, reduced barriers to entry for less sophisticated actors, and contributed to the surge in successful compromises. Automated exploitation was observed in both initial access and post-exploitation phases.

objects.name: Chaining Vulnerability Exploitation for Lateral Movement
objects.description: Threat actors demonstrated the ability to chain multiple vulnerability exploits, moving laterally within compromised environments. For example, after gaining initial access via React2Shell or legacy flaws, attackers exploited additional vulnerabilities (e.g., improper input validation, buffer overflows) in adjacent systems to escalate privileges and expand control. This multi-stage approach increased the impact and persistence of attacks, complicating incident response and remediation efforts.

objects.name: EtherRAT
objects.description: EtherRAT is a remote access trojan deployed by threat actors exploiting React2Shell, enabling attackers to remotely control compromised systems.

objects.name: PeerBlight
objects.description: PeerBlight is a backdoor malware observed in React2Shell exploitation, providing persistent access and control over infected hosts.

objects.name: CowTunnel
objects.description: CowTunnel is a backdoor used in attacks leveraging the React2Shell vulnerability, facilitating unauthorized remote access for threat actors.

objects.name: ZinFoq
objects.description: ZinFoq is a backdoor deployed during React2Shell exploitation, allowing attackers to maintain control and execute commands on compromised systems.

objects.name: Kaiji
objects.description: Kaiji and its variants are botnet/backdoor malware observed in React2Shell attacks, used for remote control and lateral movement within networks.

objects.name: Zndoor
objects.description: Zndoor is a backdoor malware leveraged in React2Shell exploitation, providing attackers with persistent access and command execution capabilities.

objects.name: RondoDox
objects.description: RondoDox is a backdoor used in React2Shell-related attacks, enabling threat actors to execute commands and maintain access to compromised systems.

objects.name: MINOCAT
objects.description: MINOCAT is a backdoor observed in React2Shell exploitation, used to provide attackers with persistent access and control over infected machines.

objects.name: SNOWLIGHT
objects.description: SNOWLIGHT is a backdoor deployed via React2Shell, facilitating command execution and remote access for threat actors.

objects.name: COMPOOD
objects.description: COMPOOD is a backdoor used in React2Shell exploitation, enabling attackers to maintain persistent access and control over compromised systems.

objects.name: HISONIC
objects.description: HISONIC is a backdoor observed in React2Shell attacks, providing threat actors with remote access and command execution capabilities.

objects.name: ANGRYREBEL.LINUX
objects.description: ANGRYREBEL.LINUX is a Linux-targeted backdoor deployed during React2Shell exploitation, granting persistent remote access to attackers.

objects.name: Weaxor
objects.description: Weaxor ransomware, observed using a Cobalt Strike stager, is deployed in React2Shell attacks to encrypt files and demand ransom from victims.

objects.name: Cobalt Strike
objects.description: Cobalt Strike is a legitimate penetration testing tool used maliciously as a stager for Weaxor ransomware and other payloads in React2Shell exploitation.

objects.name: HiddenOrbit
objects.description: HiddenOrbit relay infrastructure is used to facilitate command-and-control communications for malware deployed in React2Shell attacks.

objects.name: GobRAT
objects.description: GobRAT relay component is used for facilitating remote access and command-and-control in React2Shell-related attacks.

objects.name: AquaShell
objects.description: AquaShell is a remote access trojan deployed by UAT-9686 via Cisco Secure Email Gateway exploitation, providing attackers with remote control capabilities.

objects.name: AquaPurge
objects.description: AquaPurge is a backdoor used by UAT-9686 in Cisco Secure Email Gateway attacks, enabling persistent access and command execution.

objects.name: AquaTunnel
objects.description: AquaTunnel is a backdoor deployed by UAT-9686, facilitating covert command-and-control communications and persistent access.

objects.name: Supershell
objects.description: Supershell is a remote access trojan deployed via Gogs vulnerability exploitation, allowing attackers to remotely control compromised systems.

objects.name: Earth Lamia
objects.description: Earth Lamia is a China-nexus threat actor observed exploiting the React2Shell vulnerability (CVE-2025-55182) for espionage operations during December 2025.
goals: Leverage critical flaws for intelligence collection

objects.name: Jackpot Panda
objects.description: Jackpot Panda is a China-nexus threat actor attributed with exploiting critical vulnerabilities, including React2Shell, for espionage activities in December 2025.
goals: Leverage critical flaws for intelligence collection

objects.name: UAT-9686
objects.description: UAT-9686 is a China-nexus threat actor observed exploiting Cisco Secure Email Gateway (CVE-2025-20393) to deploy malware such as AquaShell, AquaPurge, and AquaTunnel for espionage operations.
goals: Deploy AquaShell, AquaPurge, and AquaTunnel malware

objects.name: UNC6600
objects.description: UNC6600 is a China-linked cluster observed exploiting the React2Shell vulnerability as part of coordinated espionage campaigns in December 2025.

objects.name: UNC6586
objects.description: UNC6586 is a China-linked cluster attributed with exploiting the React2Shell vulnerability for espionage activities during December 2025.

objects.name: UNC6588
objects.description: UNC6588 is a China-linked cluster observed exploiting the React2Shell vulnerability for espionage operations in December 2025.

objects.name: UNC6603
objects.description: UNC6603 is a China-linked cluster involved in exploiting the React2Shell vulnerability for espionage purposes in December 2025.

objects.name: UNC6595
objects.description: UNC6595 is a China-linked cluster attributed with exploiting the React2Shell vulnerability for espionage campaigns in December 2025.

objects.name: North Korea-linked groups
objects.description: North Korea-linked groups were observed exploiting the React2Shell vulnerability, likely for espionage or financial gain, during December 2025.
goals: Exploit React2Shell for espionage or financial gain

objects.name: Financially motivated groups
objects.description: Financially motivated groups exploited the React2Shell vulnerability, deploying ransomware and other payloads for monetary gain in December 2025.

objects.name: Unknown actors
objects.description: Unknown actors leveraged the Gogs vulnerability (CVE-2025-8110) to deploy Supershell malware, with no further attribution provided.

objects.name: Cobalt Strike
objects.description: Used by threat actors to deploy a stager for Weaxor ransomware during exploitation of the React2Shell vulnerability, enabling remote access and command-and-control capabilities.

objects.name: Gogs
objects.description: Exploited via CVE-2025-8110 (path traversal) to deploy Supershell malware, leveraging the legitimate Git server software as an entry point for initial access and execution of malicious payloads.

objects.name: MongoDB
objects.description: Abused through CVE-2025-14847 to gain unauthorized access and manipulate database contents, using the legitimate database platform for exploitation and privilege escalation.

objects.name: WinRAR
objects.description: Targeted via CVE-2025-6218 (path traversal) to execute arbitrary code or access sensitive files, abusing the legitimate archive utility for exploitation and initial access.

objects.name: React Server Components
objects.description: Exploited through CVE-2025-55182 (React2Shell) to achieve unauthenticated remote code execution, leveraging the legitimate web framework for initial access and execution of attacker-controlled code.

objects.name: Cisco Secure Email Gateway
objects.description: Abused via CVE-2025-20393 to deploy AquaShell, AquaPurge, and AquaTunnel malware, using the legitimate email gateway for exploitation and initial access.
